query: evidence on eculizumab use in myasthenia gravis

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Another treatment approach is to use immunosuppressive therapies, including corticosteroids like prednisone, as a first-line treatment to reduce autoantibody production. Other options are steroid-sparing agents, such as azathioprine or mycophenolate mofetil, and the monoclonal antibodies rituximab and eculizumab. Rituximab targets B cells to reduce the production of pathogenic autoantibodies and is particularly effective in MuSK antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. Eculizumab is a complement inhibitor and is used in AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis with refractory symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: Type II Hypersensitivity Reaction -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should be aware of the potential for medications such as fluoroquinolones, macrolides, and neuromuscular blocking agents to exacerbate myasthenia gravis. Pyridostigmine improves neuromuscular transmission and is first-line in patients with mild symptoms of myasthenia gravis. Biologics such as rozanolixizumab, IVIG, glucocorticoids, or plasma exchange are used in patients with refractory symptoms or myasthenic crisis. Thymectomy is beneficial in early-onset or thymoma-associated disease. Fc receptor antagonists, such as efgartigimod, and C5 inhibitors, such as eculizumab, represent novel targeted therapies for severe cases. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Immunosuppressive Treatment: These are indicated in patients who remain symptomatic even after pyridostigmine treatment. Glucocorticoids (prednisone, prednisolone, and methylprednisolone) and azathioprine are the first-line immunosuppressive agents used in the treatment of MG. Second-line agents include cyclosporine, methotrexate, mycophenolate, cyclophosphamide, and tacrolimus. These are used when the patient is unresponsive to treatment, has any contraindication to treatment, or intolerability to the use of first-line agents. Recently, various monoclonal antibodies, including rituximab and eculizumab, have been used to treat drug-resistant MG, but data from clinical trials about their efficacy is yet to be documented. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Plasmapheresis -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis treatment research is a rapidly developing field, with multiple new drugs approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) in the last couple of years. These novel agents include efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab, ravulizumab, and zilucoplan. Efgartigimod and rozanolixizumab are neonatal Fc receptor (FcRn) antagonists that lower pathogenic antibodies by enhancing their degradation. Rozanolixizumab is approved for both AChR and MuSK antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. Ravulizumab and zilucoplan are complement inhibitors that block complement-mediated damage at the NMJ, specifically for AChR antibody-positive myasthenia gravis. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The primary objective of initial treatment for LEMS, whether associated with malignancy or not, is to elevate ACh levels for symptomatic management. In cases of refractory weakness, the recommended first-line immunosuppressive approach involves IVIG. Other suggested alternatives include prednisone, rituximab, azathioprine, or plasma exchange.

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The mainstay of treatment in MG involves cholinesterase enzyme inhibitors and immunosuppressive agents. Symptoms that are resistant to primary treatment modalities or those requiring rapid resolution of symptoms (myasthenic crisis), plasmapheresis or intravenous immunoglobulins can be used. Management strategies in MG are based on the following four principles:

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Transmission -- Pathophysiology -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Muscle weakness in myasthenia gravis can be temporarily relieved with AChE inhibitors, such as physostigmine or neostigmine, which increase the amount of ACh in the synaptic cleft and enhance conduction through the NMJ. Corticosteroids and steroid-sparing agents, including azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, tacrolimus, and mycophenolate, are used to suppress the immune response in myasthenia gravis. Rituximab, an anti-CD20 B-cell monoclonal antibody, may be used in refractory patients, especially those with MuSK-related myasthenia gravis. Plasmapheresis is used to remove antibodies from the body, while both plasmapheresis and IV immunoglobulins are used to manage complications, including acute flare-ups, myasthenic crises, and respiratory involvement. Patients experiencing a respiratory crisis may also require ventilatory support. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Neuromuscular Junction -- Clinical Significance -- Myasthenia Gravis
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myasthenic crises can be life-threatening and are generally managed with rapid-acting treatments, including plasmapheresis and intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG), to reduce circulating autoantibodies quickly. Thymectomy is considered for patients with thymomas or even generalized myasthenia gravis with nondiseased thymus, as it can help reduce the severity of symptoms and improve long-term outcomes. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Rituximab -- Indications -- Myasthenia gravis
retrieved_docs.content: There are numerous off-label uses of rituximab. According to a retrospective cross-sectional study performed in the US, the use of rituximab for off-label indications increased from 1.2% in 2009 to 55.6% in 2017. [1] Off-label uses include refractory autoimmune hemolytic anemia, refractory myasthenia gravis, multiple sclerosis, chronic steroid-refractory GVHD, Hodgkin lymphoma, systemic lupus erythematosus, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, Waldenstrom macroglobulinemia, and membranous nephropathy.

retrieved_docs.title: Lambert-Eaton Myasthenic Syndrome -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Rituximab: This monoclonal antibody targets CD20 receptors found on B lymphocytes. Theoretically, it is effective against all B-cell-mediated diseases, including LEMS. Nevertheless, data supporting its more extensive utilization is insufficient. [16] Plasma exchange: In certain refractory cases, plasma exchange holds the potential to offer benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Edrophonium (Archived) -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Edrophonium was classically used for differentiation of cholinergic crisis from the myasthenic crisis. [2] Edrophonium briefly ameliorated the symptoms of MG by increasing the amount of acetylcholine in the NMJ synapses. The increased levels of acetylcholine in the NMJ resulted in brief improvements in skeletal and muscular strength in MG patients. Edrophonium's historical use was in MG patients with ptosis or extraocular muscle weaknesses with immediate improvement upon drug administration. As of 2018, the FDA discontinued edrophonium, which is no longer available in the United States due to its high rate of false-positive results and the development of serological antibody testing as the gold standard for diagnosing MG. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Myasthenia Gravis -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder affecting the neuromuscular junction. It manifests as a generalized muscle weakness which can involve the respiratory muscles and can lead to a myasthenic crisis, which is a medical emergency. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of myasthenia gravis, as well as the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 563
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are pharmacologic agents that belong to the therapeutic class of drugs used in hereditary angioedema. Both medications are specifically indicated for the management of hereditary angioedema attacks, targeting the underlying mechanisms of the disorder to reduce swelling and improve patient outcomes. Their inclusion in this drug class highlights their shared role in treating this rare, potentially life‑threatening condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: preparation label. The therapeutic area “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema” comprises ecallantide and icatibant, both biologic agents targeting bradykinin‑mediated angioedema. In the ATC system, aceneuramic acid, eteplirsen and nusinersen are grouped under “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system,” reflecting their application in musculo‑skeletal diseases. Nusinersen is the child of the “N [Preparations]” group and exerts a physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, which underlies its therapeutic action. Collectively, these relationships delineate a structured taxonomy that links individual drugs to ATC categories, preparation hierarchies, pharmacologic classes (including inverse‑ISA relationships), and specific therapeutic indications, thereby providing a comprehensive view of their roles in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 800
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are classified as members of the therapeutic class “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema,” and this class is a subclass of the ATC category “other hematological agents.” Heme products (pharmacologic substance) are also defined as a subclass of “other hematological agents.” Crizanlizumab is described as a selective adhesion molecule inhibitor and is linked to the disposition “Immunomodulator (disposition),” indicating that it possesses immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive properties (noted by the inverse‑ISA relationships with “imunomodulátory” and “imunosupresiva”). Consequently, crizanlizumab is a member of the “other hematological agents” ATC group. Voxelotor is likewise listed as a member of “other hematological agents.” Together, these entities illustrate the breadth of the ATC “other hematological agents” category, encompassing treatments for hereditary angioedema (ecallantide, icatibant), heme‑related pharmacologic substances, and novel agents such as crizanlizumab that act as selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitors with immunomodulatory effects, all of which are relevant for the management of various hematologic and inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: fenylefrin, cyclopentamine, nafazolin, tuamioheptane and xylometazoline; the latter two also belong to the adrenergic substance family (“adrenergní látky”) and to the plain sympathomimetic subgroup, indicating basic adrenergic activity. Efedrin is additionally a member of the “E [Preparations]” hierarchy, which also comprises enalapril, ecallantide, eprozinol and eteplirsen. Fenylefrin is classified both as a plain sympathomimetic and as a sympathomimetic used as a decongestant, reflecting its adrenergic mechanism. Therapeutically, ecallantide and icatibant are placed in the “drugs used in hereditary angioedema” class, addressing the rare bradykinin‑mediated disorder. Ecallantide’s inclusion in the “E [Preparations]” group links it to eprozinol, which is explicitly noted as not being an H1‑histamine antagonist, and to eteplirsen. retrieved_docs.content: Perfenazin is contraindicated in patients who have اضطراب خلايا البلازما (plasma cell disorder), meaning that the drug should not be prescribed to individuals with this condition to avoid potential adverse effects. This contraindication underscores the importance of reviewing a patient’s history for plasma cell disorders before initiating perfenazin therapy, ensuring safer medication selection and better patient care.

Overall, the taxonomy illustrates how individual drugs—ranging from antiallergic nasal agents, plain and decongestant sympathomimetics, adrenergic substances, H1‑histamine antagonists, to specialized therapies for hereditary angioedema and rare musculoskeletal disorders—are organized into overlapping classes that guide precise therapeutic selection and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 1 -- Community 245
retrieved_docs.content: Ecallantide and icatibant are identified as drugs used in hereditary angioedema, and both are classified as members of the therapeutic group “Drugs used in hereditary angioedema.” This drug class is itself a subclass of the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) category “other hematological agents,” linking ecallantide and icatibant to a broader group of hematology‑related medications. Heme products, described as pharmacologic substances, are also placed under the same ATC category “other hematological agents,” indicating that they share a common therapeutic classification with the hereditary angioedema drugs. Crizanlizumab is described as a therapeutic agent belonging to the class of immunological agents. It is characterized as an immunomodulator and an immunosuppressive agent, and more specifically as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor. These disposition and product descriptors reinforce its role in modulating immune and inflammatory pathways. Consistent with the classification

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 34
retrieved_docs.content: purpose and mechanism—linking blood‑forming and hemostatic agents to antithrombotic therapy, connecting systemic hormonal preparations to corticosteroids, calcium regulators, and anti‑adrenal agents, and integrating specialized hematological drugs, including hereditary angioedema therapies and enzymatic agents—thereby aiding clinicians in selecting appropriate therapies, anticipating class effects, and navigating the comprehensive ATC taxonomy for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 1 -- Community 97
retrieved_docs.content: Blood‑and‑blood‑forming organ drugs, hematological enzymes, heme‑derived pharmacologic substances, and treatments for hereditary angioedema are all classified in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other hematological agents,” making these terms interchangeable within this hierarchy. Within this group, the hemostatic effects subgroup aligns with vitamin K and other hemostatics, indicating that agents with vitamin K‑like activity belong to the same class. Specific members of the broader “agent affecting blood or body fluid” category include carglumic acid, dextrose monohydrate, and the monoclonal antibody crizanlizumab; crizanlizumab is further described as a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor, an immunological agent, and both an immunomodulatory and immunosuppressive medication. The enzyme class “deoxyribonucleases / plasmin” contains the human fibrinolysin preparation and falls under the hematological enzymes subgroup, which together with deoxyribonukleasy (e.g., streptodornase) is placed in the same ATC category. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as ecallantide and icatibant, are also members of “other hematological agents,” linking them to the same therapeutic family as the hematological enzymes and heme‑derived products. Voxelotor shares this ATC classification with crizanlizumab, underscoring that diverse agents—including hemostatics, enzyme preparations, immunomodulatory antibodies, metabolic sugars, and specific hereditary angioedema treatments—are unified under the “other hematological agents” umbrella, reflecting their common relevance to blood formation, coagulation, and immune‑mediated blood disorders and aiding clinicians in therapeutic selection and anticipation of class‑related effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 122
retrieved_docs.content: Blood and blood‑forming organ drugs constitute a subclass of other hematological agents in the ATC system and are characterized by hemostatic effects and antithrombotic activity. Within this broader group, direct Factor Xa inhibitors are identified as antithrombotic agents that act by inhibiting Factor Xa; rivaroxaban is a specific drug that belongs to this class. Other antithrombotic agents in ATC also include fondaparinux and dermatansulfát, which share the antithrombotic classification. Drugs used in hereditary angioedema, such as icatibant, are likewise placed under other hematological agents, linking the treatment of this rare swelling disorder to the same ATC category. Heme products represent additional pharmacologic substances classified as other hematological agents. Crizanlizumab exemplifies the multifunctional nature of some agents in this category. It is a selective adhesion‑molecule inhibitor that functions as both an immunomodulator and an immunosuppressive drug, and it is formally a member of other hematological agents. This places crizanlizumab alongside other agents such as voxelotor within the same ATC grouping. The hemostatic effects associated with blood‑forming organ drugs encompass vitamin K and other hemostatics, underscoring the role of these agents in promoting clot formation and preventing bleeding. Collectively, these interrelated classifications illustrate how diverse pharmacologic substances—ranging from anticoagulants and hemostatics to immunomodulators—are organized under the umbrella of other hematological agents, facilitating a coherent understanding of their mechanisms and therapeutic applications in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: members of the ATC class “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” Additional systemic agents in this class include eprozinol, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab, and tezepelumab. Eprozinol, while primarily a systemic obstructive‑airway drug, shows an inverse relationship to H1‑histamine antagonists, indicating a secondary pharmacological link to the antihistamine class. In the realm of nasal preparations, mupirocin is listed among “other nasal preparations” and is noted to produce the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis. The antisense oligonucleotide nusinersen, also classified within the “N [Preparations]” group, shares this effect and is placed in the ATC category “other drugs for disorders of the musculo‑skeletal system.” This musculo‑skeletal group further comprises palovarotene, aceneuramic acid, and eteplirsen, all sharing the same ATC categorization. Overall, the knowledge base integrates hematologic abnormalities (anemia, leukopenia, immunoglobulin deficiency), autoimmune connective‑tissue disease, related weakness and fatigue, ill‑defined symptom categories, and both therapeutic and contraindicated drugs, while highlighting the importance of recognizing transient cerebrovascular events and associated clinical terminology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2484
retrieved_docs.content: E [Preparations] is a broad category of pharmaceutical preparations that encompasses several distinct drugs, including efedrin, ecallantide, and enoxaparin. As the parent class, E [Preparations] groups these agents together, indicating that each of them—efedrin, a sympathomimetic agent; ecallantide, a kallikrein inhibitor used for hereditary angioedema; and enoxaparin, a low‑molecular‑weight heparin anticoagulant—belongs to the same overarching preparation class. This classification highlights their shared status as formulated medicinal products while recognizing their individual therapeutic roles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1870
retrieved_docs.content: Icatibant is classified among the drugs used to treat hereditary angioedema, a rare genetic disorder characterized by episodic swelling of the skin and mucosal tissues. As a bradykinin B2‑receptor antagonist, icatibant specifically blocks the action of bradykinin, a key mediator of the swelling attacks in hereditary angioedema, thereby providing rapid relief of symptoms during acute episodes. Inclusion of icatibant in the therapeutic armamentarium underscores its role as a targeted pharmacologic option for managing this condition and improving patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1622
retrieved_docs.content: N [Preparations] serves as a parent category that includes both nedokromil and nusinersen. Nedokromil is identified as a member of the class of antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, indicating its use in respiratory conditions where allergy‑mediated bronchoconstriction is a concern. Nusinersen, while also belonging to the broader N [Preparations] group, is a distinct therapeutic agent used for the treatment of spinal muscular atrophy, reflecting a neuromuscular application rather than a respiratory one. This organization demonstrates that the N [Preparations] category encompasses a range of medications with different mechanisms and clinical indications, from inhaled antiallergic agents like nedokromil to antisense oligonucleotide therapies such as nusinersen.
